heroOverview: Dicroceliasis is an infection of the bile ducts caused by the lancet liver fluke Dicrocoelium dendriticum (and rarely D. hospes), a small trematode transmitted through a unique two-land-animal lifecycle involving terrestrial snails and ants rather than aquatic hosts. Unlike the fish-borne liver flukes, infection in humans occurs by accidentally ingesting infected ants present on raw vegetables, herbs, or grass. True human infection is rare and frequently asymptomatic, with only a few hundred confirmed cases published in the medical literature; however, it is commonly overdiagnosed because of spurious parasitosis — the appearance of Dicrocoelium eggs in stool after consumption of infected animal livers. The adult worms inhabit the bile ducts and gallbladder of the definitive host, causing chronic biliary inflammation similar to but generally milder than opisthorchiasis. Praziquantel is the treatment of choice when genuine infection is confirmed.

keyStats.prevalence: True human infection is rare worldwide; fewer than 300 confirmed human cases published in peer-reviewed literature as of 2024
keyStats.demographics: Cases reported sporadically in Europe (Spain, Italy, Germany, Eastern Europe), North Africa, Asia, and the Americas; no sex predilection in confirmed reports
keyStats.avgAge: No specific age predilection; cases reported across all age groups in endemic livestock areas
keyStats.globalCases: Dicrocoelium dendriticum is common in sheep, cattle, and other ruminants worldwide; human infection follows incidental ant ingestion in rural or pastoral environments

definition: Dicroceliasis (ICD-10: B66.2) is infection with Dicrocoelium dendriticum or, rarely, D. hospes, small hermaphroditic lancet-shaped trematodes belonging to the family Dicrocoeliidae. Unlike the fish-borne liver flukes Clonorchis and Opisthorchis, Dicrocoelium uses a fully terrestrial transmission cycle with two intermediate hosts: land snails (principally Cionella lubrica and related species in Europe) and ants (principally Formica species) as second intermediate hosts. Adult flukes (6-10 mm long) reside in the small bile ducts and gallbladder of the definitive hosts — primarily sheep, cattle, deer, rabbits, and other herbivores worldwide. Humans are incidental dead-end hosts, acquiring infection by accidentally swallowing infected ants on contaminated raw vegetables, herbs, grass, or fresh animal fodder. After ingestion, metacercariae excyst and migrate up the bile duct, maturing into adults within 7-10 weeks. The clinical significance of dicroceliasis in humans is a subject of ongoing discussion: most confirmed cases have mild or absent biliary symptoms, though chronic heavy infection has been reported to cause cholangitis, cholecystitis, and liver enzyme abnormalities. A critical diagnostic caveat is spurious parasitosis (pseudo-parasitosis): transit of Dicrocoelium eggs through the gut after eating infected raw or undercooked animal liver (typically sheep or cattle liver) produces eggs in stool without true infection, easily mistaken for genuine dicroceliasis. Genuine infection requires demonstration of eggs in stool after excluding raw liver consumption and, ideally, confirmation with a repeat stool exam after several days on a liver-free diet.

typesClassification.description: Genuine infection with adult D. dendriticum in the bile ducts; rare in humans; confirmed by persistent egg shedding on liver-free diet; may cause mild biliary symptoms or be completely asymptomatic.

Passage of Dicrocoelium eggs in stool after ingestion of raw or undercooked infected animal liver; the eggs transit without infection occurring; the most common cause of Dicrocoelium eggs found in human stool; identified by eliminating raw liver from the diet for 48 hours before a repeat stool exam.

Rarely, heavy worm burdens cause persistent right upper quadrant pain, dyspepsia, liver enzyme elevations, and biliary abnormalities on imaging; this form is more likely in patients with repeated or prolonged exposure in agricultural settings.

primarySymptoms: Right upper quadrant or epigastric abdominal discomfort — typically a mild dull ache or pressure under the right rib margin from biliary inflammation in genuine infection.
Intermittent nausea and bloating, particularly after fatty meals, reflecting bile flow impairment from fluke colonisation of the distal bile ducts.
Dyspepsia and early satiety, related to impaired gallbladder contractility and biliary stasis.
Mild fatigue and general malaise in heavier infections, from the low-grade inflammatory response to worm antigens.
Intermittent mild jaundice in cases with significant worm burden obstructing bile flow, though this is uncommon in human dicroceliasis.
Hepatomegaly — a palpably enlarged liver — in heavy or long-standing infections, reflecting periductal inflammation.
Fever and right upper quadrant pain if secondary bacterial cholangitis develops as a complication of biliary obstruction.
Mild eosinophilia on blood count, typically 5-20% rather than the higher levels seen in acute fascioliasis or opisthorchiasis during tissue migration.
Pruritus (itching) from mild cholestasis during exacerbations of biliary obstruction.
Loose stools or mild diarrhoea during symptomatic phases, from altered biliary and intestinal motility.
earlyWarningSigns: Mild, intermittent right-sided upper abdominal discomfort in a person working in agriculture or livestock husbandry in endemic areas.
Finding of Dicrocoelium eggs on stool examination, which requires differentiation from spurious parasitosis before concluding genuine infection.
Mild eosinophilia on a blood count in a person from a sheep or cattle farming background without another identified cause.
Vague dyspepsia that does not respond to standard dietary modification in someone with regular contact with ruminant grazing land.
emergencySigns: Fever with jaundice and severe right upper quadrant pain — Charcot's triad indicating acute bacterial cholangitis — requires emergency antibiotics and biliary drainage regardless of underlying parasitic cause.
Sudden severe abdominal pain with peritoneal signs suggesting gallbladder perforation.
Progressive jaundice with pale stools and dark urine indicating biliary obstruction needing urgent evaluation.
Septic shock with confusion and low blood pressure in a patient with biliary disease — Reynold's pentad representing severe ascending cholangitis.

causes.cause: Accidental ingestion of infected ants on raw vegetation
causes.description: The primary route of human infection: D. dendriticum metacercariae are concentrated in the head ganglia of infected Formica ants (sometimes making ants behave abnormally, remaining on vegetation rather than returning underground). Humans accidentally swallow infected ants while eating raw salads, herbs, watercress, unwashed wild greens, or fresh fodder. The ingestion is invariably unintentional.

causes.cause: Fully terrestrial life cycle through snails and ants
causes.description: D. dendriticum eggs passed in ruminant faeces are swallowed by land snails, which shed infective cercariae in slime balls on vegetation. Ants ingest the slime balls and become infected second intermediate hosts. When humans or other definitive hosts eat infected ants, metacercariae are released in the duodenum and migrate up the bile duct to mature into adults.

People who work with or live near sheep, cattle, and deer on pasture land where D. dendriticum is endemic in the livestock population are most at risk of incidental ant ingestion. Direct contact with infected animal faeces or liver does not transmit the parasite — only ingestion of infected ants causes true infection.

causes.cause: Spurious egg passage from eating infected raw liver
causes.description: Ingestion of raw or undercooked sheep or cattle liver containing D. dendriticum eggs results in egg transit through the human gut, producing a stool sample positive for eggs without true infection. This is the most common mechanism for 'dicroceliasis' being reported in clinical settings, and it must be excluded before treating a patient.

causes.cause: Global ruminant reservoir sustaining the life cycle
causes.description: D. dendriticum infects sheep, cattle, deer, rabbits, and other ruminants worldwide — infection rates of 20-90% in sheep in hyperendemic grazing areas of Europe, North Africa, and Asia. This large animal reservoir maintains environmental snail and ant infection, creating ongoing human exposure risk in pastoral communities.

riskFactors.description: Farmers, shepherds, and agricultural workers regularly working on pasture land grazed by infected ruminants have the highest incidental ant ingestion risk. Kneeling on grass, eating outdoors, or working with fodder increases the likelihood of swallowing an infected ant.

riskFactors.factor: Consumption of unwashed raw greens from endemic pasture land
riskFactors.description: Wild herbs, watercress, or salad greens harvested from or near grazing land may carry infected ants. Thorough washing and inspection of raw vegetables before consumption reduces risk.

riskFactors.factor: Residence in regions with high ruminant endemicity
riskFactors.description: Parts of Spain, Italy, Germany, Eastern Europe, North Africa, Iran, Central Asia, South America, and North America where D. dendriticum infection rates in sheep and cattle are high create the environmental conditions for human exposure.

riskFactors.factor: Eating raw or undercooked animal liver (spurious risk)
riskFactors.description: Consuming raw or lightly cooked sheep or cattle liver from infected animals causes spurious egg passage, which while not a true infection leads to an incorrect diagnosis of dicroceliasis if the dietary history is not elicited. Avoiding raw liver eliminates this diagnostic confusion.

riskFactors.factor: Children playing outdoors on pasture land
riskFactors.description: Young children who play on grass in endemic areas, put soil or vegetation in their mouths, or eat fruit or berries without washing near grazing land may be more likely to ingest infected ants than adults, though published paediatric case series are sparse.

As with other helminth infections, immunocompromised individuals may develop heavier worm burdens and more symptomatic biliary disease after infection, though data specific to dicroceliasis are limited.

affectedDemographics: Farmers, shepherds, and agricultural workers in rural areas with endemic ruminant dicroceliasis in Europe, North Africa, and Asia
Inhabitants of pastoral communities in Spain, Italy, Romania, Iran, and parts of North Africa
People who eat unwashed raw wild greens harvested near livestock grazing land
Individuals whose stool contains Dicrocoelium eggs after eating raw animal liver — the spurious presentation, not true infection
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosing genuine dicroceliasis requires careful distinction from spurious parasitosis, which is by far the more common finding of Dicrocoelium eggs in human stool. When eggs are first identified, the clinician must ask specifically whether the patient has eaten raw or undercooked sheep, goat, or cattle liver in the preceding 72 hours. If so, the patient is placed on a liver-free diet for 48-72 hours and stool re-examined; persistent egg shedding after this dietary exclusion supports genuine infection, while disappearance of eggs confirms spurious passage. For genuine infection, the Kato-Katz thick smear or formalin-ether concentration technique on fresh stool is the primary diagnostic test. Dicrocoelium eggs are slightly smaller than other liver fluke eggs (38-45 µm long by 22-30 µm wide), dark brown, operculated, and contain a mature miracidium when freshly passed. They are differentiated from Clonorchis and Opisthorchis eggs by their larger size, darker colour, and the presence of a visible miracidium. Three stool samples collected on different days improve sensitivity. Serology (antibody detection by ELISA) for Dicrocoelium antigens is available in some reference laboratories and can support diagnosis in light infections or when stool tests are equivocal, though cross-reactions with other trematode antigens occur. Abdominal ultrasound is appropriate in symptomatic patients with confirmed genuine infection to assess bile duct dilation, gallbladder wall thickening, and biliary sludge. Blood tests in genuine infection may show mild eosinophilia (typically lower than in fascioliasis), and elevated alkaline phosphatase or gamma-GT in cholestatic disease. Liver biopsy is rarely needed but confirms the diagnosis histologically when flukes or fluke eggs are identified in periportal or periductal tissue. Because the infection is so rare in humans, clinical suspicion in a patient with biliary symptoms, eosinophilia, and livestock or pastoral exposure is the critical diagnostic trigger.

diagnosticTests.test: Stool microscopy with dietary exclusion (Kato-Katz or FECT)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies Dicrocoelium eggs in stool and, after a 48-72 hour raw liver-free diet, distinguishes genuine infection from spurious egg passage — the most important diagnostic step.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Collect fresh stool samples on 3 separate days; a laboratory prepares concentrated preparations and examines under a light microscope. Eggs are described and the clinician is told whether the patient consumed raw animal liver recently.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects host antibodies against D. dendriticum antigens; useful to support genuine infection in light worm burdens where egg shedding is sporadic and stool microscopy may be falsely negative.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A blood draw; results from a reference or parasitology laboratory in 2-5 days. Cross-reactions with Fasciola, Opisthorchis, and other trematodes reduce specificity; results are interpreted with clinical context.

diagnosticTests.test: Complete blood count with eosinophil differential
diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects peripheral eosinophilia; typically mild (5-20%) in dicroceliasis compared with the higher levels of acute fascioliasis; useful as a supportive finding in symptomatic patients.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw; available within 1-2 hours. Eosinophilia resolves after treatment, making it a useful monitoring marker.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Assesses biliary tract changes in symptomatic confirmed dicroceliasis — bile duct dilation, gallbladder wall thickening, and biliary sludge; excludes other causes of right upper quadrant pain.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Non-invasive gel-on-skin scan taking 15-20 minutes; fasting for 4-6 hours beforehand gives the best gallbladder assessment.

diagnosticTests.test: Liver function tests (ALP, GGT, bilirubin, ALT)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies cholestatic and hepatocellular injury in symptomatic genuine infection; helps monitor treatment response and screen for secondary bacterial cholangitis.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw processed within 1-2 hours; elevated ALP and GGT are the most characteristic findings in biliary dicroceliasis.

diagnosticTests.test: Dietary history and liver intake assessment
diagnosticTests.purpose: Distinguishes genuine infection from spurious egg passage — the most common cause of Dicrocoelium eggs in human stool — through a structured dietary history preceding the stool test.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Clinical interview; the examiner asks specifically about raw or undercooked sheep, goat, or cattle liver in the preceding 72 hours. This is a critical step requiring no laboratory equipment.

treatmentOverview: When genuine dicroceliasis is confirmed — after excluding spurious parasitosis through dietary history and repeat stool testing — treatment with praziquantel is the standard approach. The most commonly used regimen in case reports is 25 mg/kg three times in one day (75 mg/kg total), the same protocol used for other biliary trematode infections, or 40 mg/kg as a single dose; however, because genuine human dicroceliasis is so rare, there are no controlled trials and the evidence is based on scattered case reports and animal studies. Cure rates of approximately 70-100% have been reported in published case series, with egg clearance confirmed on follow-up stool examination 4-6 weeks after treatment. Albendazole at 400 mg twice daily for 7 days has been used as an alternative with variable reported efficacy. Side effects of praziquantel in dicroceliasis treatment are similar to other indications: transient dizziness, nausea, and mild abdominal cramping lasting a few hours. Because the majority of apparent dicroceliasis cases are actually spurious parasitosis, the most important treatment step is recognising the spurious nature and providing dietary counselling to avoid raw animal liver — no antiparasitic treatment is needed in this scenario. For the rare confirmed true infection with biliary complications, antiparasitic therapy is combined with standard management of cholangitis or cholecystitis as appropriate. Re-infection requires ongoing source exposure (continued ingestion of infected ants), which typically resolves with environmental avoidance measures.

medicalTreatments.name: Praziquantel (single-day three-dose regimen)
medicalTreatments.description: The drug of choice for genuine dicroceliasis; given as 25 mg/kg three times in one day (75 mg/kg total) with food, or 40 mg/kg as a single dose. Causes spastic paralysis and tegumental damage in Dicrocoelium flukes. Based on case reports and animal models rather than controlled trials.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Egg clearance reported in approximately 70-100% of genuine human cases in published case series; the limited evidence base reflects the rarity of confirmed human infection.

medicalTreatments.name: Albendazole (7-day course)
medicalTreatments.description: An alternative benzimidazole anthelmintic at 400 mg twice daily for 7 days used when praziquantel is unavailable. Some published cases report egg clearance; others have not. Also treats co-infecting soil-transmitted helminths.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Variable results in scattered case reports; approximately 50-70% egg clearance in the few published human cases using albendazole for dicroceliasis.

medicalTreatments.name: Antibiotics for secondary bacterial cholangitis
medicalTreatments.description: When biliary obstruction from a heavy worm burden causes secondary bacterial cholangitis, broad-spectrum antibiotics covering enteric gram-negative bacteria — ceftriaxone, piperacillin-tazobactam, or ciprofloxacin — are given alongside antiparasitic treatment and biliary drainage if needed.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Standard antibiotic regimens achieve clinical resolution in approximately 80-90% of mild to moderate bacterial cholangitis episodes when combined with adequate biliary drainage.

medicalTreatments.name: Dietary counselling for spurious parasitosis
medicalTreatments.description: For patients whose stool tests return positive for Dicrocoelium eggs following raw animal liver consumption, the management is education and avoidance of raw ruminant liver — no antiparasitic drug is needed. This is the most common intervention required for apparent dicroceliasis cases.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Elimination of raw liver from the diet results in immediate disappearance of eggs from subsequent stool samples; 100% effective for this diagnosis.

surgicalOptions.name: Cholecystectomy
surgicalOptions.description: Removal of the gallbladder in the uncommon scenario of recurrent cholecystitis or symptomatic gallstones caused or aggravated by chronic dicroceliasis. Performed laparoscopically in most cases after antiparasitic treatment.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Laparoscopic cholecystectomy resolves recurrent cholecystitis in approximately 95% of elective cases; complication rate below 3% in experienced hands.

surgicalOptions.name: ERCP for biliary decompression
surgicalOptions.description: Endoscopic biliary decompression is indicated if heavy worm burden causes significant biliary obstruction not resolving with medical treatment; also provides opportunity for bile duct stone extraction.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Technical success for biliary decompression exceeds 85-90% at experienced endoscopy centres.

alternativeTreatments.name: Environmental avoidance of infected ants
alternativeTreatments.description: Washing all raw vegetables, herbs, and wild greens thoroughly under running water before consumption removes ants and reduces exposure risk. Eating indoors and inspecting food before consumption in endemic agricultural areas are practical preventive measures requiring no medical intervention.

alternativeTreatments.name: Avoidance of raw ruminant liver
alternativeTreatments.description: Ensuring that all liver (sheep, cattle, goat) is fully cooked before consumption eliminates the most common cause of Dicrocoelium egg passage in humans (spurious parasitosis) and is the simplest and most immediately actionable patient education step.

whySeeSpecialist: Most genuine dicroceliasis cases can be managed by a primary care physician or an infectious disease or tropical medicine specialist who can correctly distinguish true infection from spurious parasitosis and prescribe praziquantel appropriately. A gastroenterologist should be involved when biliary complications arise — cholangitis, stone formation, or biliary strictures. In the rare scenario of heavy infection with refractory biliary disease, a hepatobiliary surgeon may be needed. For any clinician who encounters Dicrocoelium eggs in a patient's stool, consultation with a parasitologist or infectious disease specialist is advisable because of the complexity of ruling out spurious egg passage and confirming genuine infection.
doctorSelectionGuide: Because true human dicroceliasis is rare, seek an infectious disease physician or tropical medicine specialist with experience in parasitic infections of the biliary tract. A parasitology reference laboratory familiar with Dicrocoelium egg morphology and the spurious parasitosis distinction is essential for accurate diagnosis. Gastroenterologists and hepatologists are appropriate partners for evaluating biliary complications and performing ERCP when needed. In endemic countries, public health laboratories have greater familiarity with the condition.
preventionStrategies: Wash all raw vegetables, herbs, and wild greens thoroughly under running water before consumption to remove insects, including infected ants.
Avoid eating raw, unwashed plants directly from grazing land or areas where infected ruminants defaecate.
Cook all ruminant liver (sheep, cattle, goat) thoroughly before eating — this prevents spurious egg passage and eliminates any theoretical risk from handling infected liver.
Wear protective clothing and wash hands after agricultural work involving exposure to ruminant pasture, fresh fodder, or animal faeces.
Educate farmers and pastoral workers about the lifecycle of D. dendriticum and the importance of hygienic food preparation practices.
Veterinary control programmes that treat infected livestock with anthelmintics can reduce the environmental reservoir in highly endemic pastoral areas.
lifestyleModifications: Always wash raw salads and vegetables purchased near or from livestock-farming areas before eating.
Inspect and wash wild herbs or berries collected from pastures and rural areas before consuming them.
Ensure all animal liver products are fully cooked (no pink colour, internal temperature above 70°C) before serving.
After a confirmed genuine diagnosis, return for follow-up stool examination at 4-6 weeks to confirm parasitological cure.
If working in agriculture with ruminants in an endemic area, maintain awareness that raw grazing plants may carry infected ants and adopt habitual food washing.

recommended: All vegetables and greens washed thoroughly under running water before consumption
Fully cooked liver and other offal from ruminant animals
A balanced diet without specific restrictions beyond the food hygiene precautions above
avoid: Raw or undercooked sheep, goat, or cattle liver — the main cause of spurious dicroceliasis
Unwashed wild greens, watercress, or herbs harvested from grazing land in endemic regions
Eating raw vegetation directly from pasture land without thorough washing

exerciseGuidelines: No specific exercise restrictions apply to dicroceliasis. Regular physical activity is encouraged throughout. During an episode of bacterial cholangitis — with fever, jaundice, and abdominal pain — rest and medical treatment take priority. After successful treatment and confirmed parasitological cure, normal activity can be fully resumed.
dailyManagement: Before accepting a diagnosis of dicroceliasis, confirm the dietary history: did you eat raw animal liver in the past 3 days? If yes, the eggs in your stool may reflect transit without infection — a re-test after 48 hours without raw liver is required.
Take praziquantel with food on the treatment day; plan to rest or reduce demanding activities for 24 hours as dizziness is a common transient side effect.
Return for stool examination 4-6 weeks after treatment to confirm egg clearance; persistent eggs may indicate treatment failure or re-exposure.
Adopt consistent food hygiene: wash all greens and vegetables and cook all liver products before eating, as part of permanent daily practice in endemic areas.
Seek medical review promptly if fever with right-sided abdominal pain and jaundice develops — these are signs of biliary complication requiring urgent assessment.
prognosis: The prognosis of genuine dicroceliasis treated promptly with praziquantel is excellent. Published case reports document egg clearance in approximately 70-100% of confirmed cases, with resolution of any biliary symptoms within weeks. The infection carries far less oncogenic risk than Clonorchis or Opisthorchis: no association between D. dendriticum and cholangiocarcinoma has been established in the small number of human cases documented. Biliary complications are uncommon and generally resolve with antiparasitic therapy and standard management of cholangitis. Re-infection requires re-exposure to infected ants, which can be minimised through food hygiene. For the vast majority of patients whose stool is found to contain Dicrocoelium eggs after eating raw ruminant liver (spurious parasitosis), the prognosis is entirely benign and no treatment is needed beyond dietary advice. The main challenge in clinical practice is accurate diagnosis — distinguishing the common spurious presentation from the rare genuine infection — rather than the severity of the genuine disease itself.
recoveryTimeline: After praziquantel treatment for confirmed genuine dicroceliasis, Dicrocoelium eggs should clear from the stool within 4 to 6 weeks. Biliary symptoms, if present, typically improve within 2 to 4 weeks. Any mild eosinophilia normalises within 1 to 3 months. Liver enzyme abnormalities from biliary inflammation improve over 3 to 6 months after worm eradication. For spurious parasitosis, there is nothing to treat — eggs disappear from stool spontaneously within 24 to 72 hours of stopping raw liver consumption.
complications: Bacterial cholangitis — secondary bacterial infection of inflamed bile ducts in heavy genuine infection; rare but requires urgent antibiotic treatment and biliary drainage.
Cholecystitis and gallstones — biliary stasis from fluke-related dysfunction can promote gallstone formation and gallbladder inflammation.
Biliary fibrosis — very rare and only documented in exceptionally heavy long-term infections or in immunocompromised hosts; not established as a risk in typical human cases.

Diagnostic error and unnecessary treatment — the most common complication in clinical practice; spurious dicroceliasis is frequently misidentified as genuine infection, leading to inappropriate antiparasitic prescribing.

Anxiety and health concern from misdiagnosis — patients told they have a liver parasite before spurious parasitosis has been excluded may experience significant distress; accurate diagnosis is protective.

supportResources.name: European Centre for Disease Prevention and Control — Helminth Infections
supportResources.description: ECDC resource covering helminth infections including food-borne trematodes in European epidemiological context.

sources.title: Keiser J, Utzinger J. Food-borne Trematodiases. Clinical Microbiology Reviews, 2009
Bouchet F et al. Dicrocoelium in humans — historical and parasitological review. Parasite, 1999

confusedWithConditions.name: Fascioliasis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Fasciola hepatica is a much larger fluke (25-30 mm) causing an acute febrile illness with very high eosinophilia acquired from aquatic plants. Fascioliasis requires triclabendazole; dicroceliasis is treated with praziquantel. Fasciola eggs are much larger and lack the dark colour of Dicrocoelium eggs.

confusedWithConditions.name: Opisthorchiasis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Opisthorchis species are transmitted by eating raw freshwater fish, causing more significant biliary disease and cholangiocarcinoma risk. Dicrocoelium is transmitted by ant ingestion. Opisthorchis eggs are smaller and lighter than Dicrocoelium eggs.

confusedWithConditions.name: Clonorchiasis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Clonorchis sinensis is also a fish-borne liver fluke with near-identical egg morphology issues, but its geographic distribution (East Asia) and fish transmission route clearly differ from Dicrocoelium's terrestrial ant route in European and North African pastoral environments.

confusedWithConditions.name: Bacterial cholangitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Primary bacterial cholangitis presents with similar fever, jaundice, and right upper quadrant pain but lacks the pastoral/agricultural exposure history and parasite eggs in stool that define dicroceliasis. The two can coexist when dicroceliasis is the underlying cause of biliary obstruction.

relatedConditions.name: Fascioliasis
relatedConditions.relevance: Another food-borne trematode infecting bile ducts in the same geographic regions; Fasciola hepatica causes a more acute and severe illness and requires different treatment (triclabendazole).

relatedConditions.name: Opisthorchiasis
relatedConditions.relevance: The most clinically similar liver fluke infection — both inhabit bile ducts — but transmitted by fish rather than ants; both treated with praziquantel.

relatedConditions.name: Clonorchiasis
relatedConditions.relevance: Another food-borne liver fluke with overlapping biliary pathology and praziquantel treatment; distinguished by geographic distribution and fish (not ant) transmission.

relatedConditions.name: Echinococcosis
relatedConditions.relevance: Another zoonotic parasitic infection affecting the liver and biliary system in the same agricultural and pastoral communities as dicroceliasis.

faqs.question: What is dicroceliasis?
faqs.answer: Dicroceliasis is a rare parasitic infection of the bile ducts caused by the lancet liver fluke Dicrocoelium dendriticum. Unlike fish-borne liver flukes, it is transmitted by accidentally swallowing infected ants on raw vegetables. Most human infections are mild or cause no symptoms at all.

faqs.question: How do you get dicroceliasis?
faqs.answer: Dicroceliasis is acquired by accidentally swallowing small infected ants present on raw vegetables, herbs, or wild greens. The parasite uses a lifecycle involving land snails and ants — not water. Drinking water, eating cooked food, or contact with an infected person does not transmit it.

faqs.question: What is spurious dicroceliasis?
faqs.answer: Spurious dicroceliasis means Dicrocoelium eggs appear in your stool because you ate raw or undercooked animal liver (sheep, cattle, goat) that contained the parasite's eggs. The eggs simply pass through without causing infection. It is far more common than true dicroceliasis and requires no treatment — only a dietary change.

faqs.question: How is dicroceliasis diagnosed?
faqs.answer: The first step is stool microscopy to detect eggs. If eggs are found, the doctor asks whether you ate raw animal liver recently. You stop eating raw liver for 48-72 hours, then repeat the stool test. Persistent eggs after this dietary exclusion suggest genuine infection. Blood tests may show mild eosinophilia.

faqs.question: How is dicroceliasis treated?
faqs.answer: Genuine dicroceliasis is treated with praziquantel, usually 25 mg/kg three times in one day with food. Most reported cases clear eggs from the stool within 4-6 weeks. If the eggs found were due to eating raw liver (spurious dicroceliasis), no antiparasitic treatment is needed — just avoid raw liver.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of dicroceliasis?
faqs.answer: Most genuine dicroceliasis infections cause no symptoms. When symptoms occur they include mild right-sided abdominal discomfort, nausea, bloating, fatigue, and occasionally low-grade fever. Severe symptoms like jaundice and high fever suggest biliary complications and need urgent medical assessment.

faqs.question: Is dicroceliasis common?
faqs.answer: True human infection is very rare — fewer than 300 confirmed cases have been published worldwide. However, apparent dicroceliasis (spurious parasitosis from eating infected raw liver) is more frequently encountered in areas where raw animal offal is consumed. The condition is more common in sheep and cattle than in humans.

faqs.question: Where is dicroceliasis found?
faqs.answer: Dicrocoelium dendriticum infects ruminants (sheep, cattle, deer) on every inhabited continent. Human cases are reported most often in Spain, Italy, Germany, Eastern Europe, Turkey, North Africa, Iran, and Central Asia — areas with high prevalence in livestock and agricultural communities.

faqs.question: Can dicroceliasis cause cancer?
faqs.answer: No established link between Dicrocoelium dendriticum and bile duct cancer has been found in humans, unlike the fish-borne liver flukes Clonorchis and Opisthorchis. Given the rarity of confirmed human cases, long-term oncogenic risk has not been characterised, but it is not considered a significant cancer risk based on available evidence.

faqs.question: How long do Dicrocoelium flukes survive in humans?
faqs.answer: Adult Dicrocoelium flukes can survive in the bile ducts for years in animal hosts. The survival time in human hosts is not well characterised because most confirmed cases are treated and the natural history in untreated humans is poorly documented, given how rarely the infection is diagnosed.

faqs.question: How is dicroceliasis different from fascioliasis?
faqs.answer: Fascioliasis is caused by a much larger liver fluke (Fasciola hepatica, up to 30 mm) acquired by eating watercress or aquatic plants — not ants. Fascioliasis causes an acute febrile illness with very high eosinophilia and hepatitis. It requires triclabendazole for treatment, while dicroceliasis is treated with praziquantel.

faqs.question: Can children get dicroceliasis?
faqs.answer: Yes. Children who play on pasture land, eat unwashed wild fruits or berries, or put soil or plants in their mouths can accidentally ingest infected ants. However, paediatric cases are rarely published, likely reflecting both the rarity of true infection and the difficulty of diagnosing it in children.

faqs.question: What should I do if my stool test shows Dicrocoelium eggs?
faqs.answer: Do not panic — in most cases, the eggs come from eating raw or undercooked animal liver (spurious parasitosis). Tell your doctor whether you ate raw liver in the last three days. If yes, stop eating raw liver and repeat the stool test after two to three days. If eggs persist, you may have genuine infection and should discuss treatment with your doctor.

faqs.question: Is there a risk of dicroceliasis from pet contact?
faqs.answer: Cats and dogs can be infected with Dicrocoelium in endemic areas, but direct contact with an infected pet does not transmit the parasite to humans. Infection requires ingesting an infected ant. Basic hand hygiene after outdoor contact with animals and their environments remains good practice.

faqs.question: How can I prevent dicroceliasis?
faqs.answer: Wash all raw vegetables, herbs, and wild greens thoroughly before eating to remove ants and other insects. Cook all ruminant liver fully before serving. When working in pastoral areas in endemic regions, avoid eating unwashed plants or fruits directly from the ground. These simple measures effectively prevent most exposure.

faqs.question: Does dicroceliasis affect animals more than humans?
faqs.answer: Yes. Dicrocoelium dendriticum is a well-recognised veterinary pathogen causing liver disease and economic losses in sheep and cattle worldwide. Human infection is incidental and rare. Veterinary control programmes using anthelmintics in livestock can reduce environmental contamination and indirectly lower human exposure risk.

faqs.question: Why is the Dicrocoelium lifecycle unusual compared to other liver flukes?
faqs.answer: Most liver flukes (Clonorchis, Opisthorchis, Fasciola) use water-based intermediate hosts — snails and fish or aquatic plants. Dicrocoelium uses a fully terrestrial cycle: land snails as the first intermediate host and ants as the second, making it unique among liver flukes in not requiring any aquatic exposure for transmission.

faqs.question: Can cooking kill Dicrocoelium larvae in food?
faqs.answer: Heating food to above 60-70°C destroys Dicrocoelium larvae and eggs. Fully cooking animal liver eliminates the risk of spurious egg passage. Thoroughly washing raw vegetables removes insects including infected ants. Normal cooking temperatures used for liver dishes are sufficient to destroy any parasitic material.

faqs.question: What blood test results suggest dicroceliasis?
faqs.answer: Mild peripheral eosinophilia (typically 5-20% of white blood cells) is the most consistent blood test finding in genuine dicroceliasis. Alkaline phosphatase and GGT may be mildly elevated in biliary disease. Eosinophilia is lower than typically seen in fascioliasis and may be absent in light or chronic infections.

faqs.question: Is re-infection with dicroceliasis common after treatment?
faqs.answer: Re-infection is possible but uncommon if hygienic food preparation habits are adopted. Unlike fish-borne liver flukes in hyperendemic communities, the environmental exposure to infected ants in pastoral areas is generally intermittent. Washing raw vegetables consistently and avoiding raw liver are effective long-term preventive measures.

metaDescription: Dicroceliasis is a rare bile duct infection from the lancet liver fluke, often confused with spurious egg passage from raw liver. Learn symptoms and treatment.
